---
title: "Lesson 3: The Gamma Distribution"
subtitle: "Statistical Estimation for Data Science and AI"
---

::: {.column-margin}
{{< video 01_the-gamma-distribution.mp4 >}}

[transcript](01_the-gamma-distribution.en.txt){target="_blank"}
:::

### Handout

[Handout](02_video-slides-the-gamma-distribution_Gamma_Distribution_Slides.pdf){target="_blank"}


::: {#def-gamma-distribution}
## Gamma Distribution
The continuous random variable $X$ is said to have a gamma distribution with parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$ if $X$ has pdf

$$
f(x) = \frac{1}{\Gamma(\alpha)} \beta^\alpha x^{\alpha-1} e^{-\beta x} \mathbf{1}_{(0,\infty)}(x)
$$ {#eq-gamma-pdf}

- with: 
    - $\alpha > 0$ is the *shape* parameter and 
    - $\beta > 0$ is the *inverse scale* or *rate* parameter. 
- which we denote with $X \sim \Gamma(\alpha, \beta)$.
:::

Not surprisingly, different values of the shape parameter lead to different shapes of the distribution.

```{r}
#| label: fig-gamma-shapes
#| fig-cap: "Gamma distributions with different shape parameters"
#| fig-subcap:
#|   - "Shape = 0.5 - assymptotic at 0"
#|   - "Shape = 1 - exponential"
#|   - "Shape = 5 - hump-shaped"
#| layout-ncol: 3
#| warning: false
#| message: false

library(ggplot2)

x <- seq(0.001, 12, length.out = 1000)

gamma_plot <- function(shape, rate = 1) {
  data.frame(
    x = x,
    density = dgamma(x, shape = shape, rate = rate)
  ) |>
    ggplot(aes(x, density)) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
    labs(
      title = paste0("Gamma(shape = ", shape, ", rate = ", rate, ")"),
      x = "x",
      y = "Density"
    ) +
    theme_minimal()
}

gamma_plot(shape = 0.5)
gamma_plot(shape = 1)
gamma_plot(shape = 5)
```


### Reflections

::: {.callout-tip}
## Learn to :heart: the $\Beta(\alpha, \beta)$ distribution.

- Beta
    - is an enhanced version of the Binomial Distribution that works for continuous data on the interval [0,1], 
    - has a uniform parameterization option,
    - Is the conjugate prior for the Bernoulli and Binomial distributions in Bayesian statistics. 
    - It is thus useful for counting problems where we want to estimate proportions of successes to failures like in A/B testing.
    - Beta is also the building block for the Dirichlet distribution which is used in many non-parametric models based on the Dirichlet process.
:::


::: {.callout-tip}
## Learn to :heart: the $\Gamma(a,b)$ distribution.

- $\Gamma(a,b)$ has positive values and is thus useful for modeling waiting times, lifetimes, and other positive-valued quantities. For this reason it is often seen as the prior for an unknown variance in the Normal distribution when the mean is known.
- Gamma can be transformed into a Chi-Squared distribution which is used in many statistical tests used in hypothesis testing and model evaluation.
- It is also the conjugate prior for the Exponential distribution in Bayesian statistics.
- It is also used as a prior for Normal variance when the mean is known.
- If you are lucky You will also get to make use of it for non-parametric models based on the  dirichlet process where it is used as a building block for the stick-breaking construction of the dirichlet process.
:::

In my notes on the gamma distribution from a previous course I got confused between the `Gamma function` and the `Gamma distribution`. The professor kindly clarified the difference for us:

- **one parameter** indicates the *function* that generalizes the factorial function to non-integer values.
- **two parameters** indicates the *distribution* defined by two parameters, often denoted as $\alpha$ (shape) and $\beta$ (rate or scale).



Also The **beta** and **gamma** distributions are closely related through normalization.

If

$$
X \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha,\lambda), \qquad
Y \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\beta,\lambda)
$$

independently, with the **same rate** (\lambda), then

$$
\frac{X}{X+Y} \sim \mathrm{Beta}(\alpha,\beta).
$$

So a beta-distributed random variable can be constructed as the **proportion** of one gamma random variable in the sum of two independent gamma random variables.

Moreover,

$$
X+Y \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha+\beta,\lambda),
$$

and the ratio

$$
\frac{X}{X+Y}
$$

is independent of the sum (X+Y).

This generalizes: if

$$
X_i \sim \mathrm{Gamma}(\alpha_i,\lambda)
$$

independently for (i=1,\dots,k), then

$$
\left(\frac{X_1}{\sum_j X_j},\dots,\frac{X_k}{\sum_j X_j}\right)
\sim \mathrm{Dirichlet}(\alpha_1,\dots,\alpha_k).
$$
The beta distribution is therefore the two-dimensional case of a normalized gamma construction; the Dirichlet distribution is the multivariate version.

This relationship is central in Bayesian modeling: gamma variables model positive masses or intensities, while beta and Dirichlet variables model normalized proportions.
